The Grasberg Mine in Indonesia, one of the world’s largest open-pit mining complexes, presents unique challenges due to its steep topography, tropical climate, and intensive excavation. In this study, surface displacement from 2017 to July 2025 was analyzed using ascending and descending C-band Sentinel-1 InSAR data, corrected for atmospheric delays with ERA5 reanalysis. Results show cumulative subsidence at selected points around -20 cm in the ascending track, while the descending track reveals uplift of approximately 17 cm. Decomposition of the displacement field highlights opposing east-west motions on opposite pit flanks, indicating mass movement and stress distribution converging into the Pit and confirming typical contraction patterns in the Open-Pit mining environments. Temporal change detection using the Pruned Exact Linear Time algorithm identified 12 distinct deformation phases, reflecting the episodic nature of mining-induced ground motion. These findings emphasize the importance of multigeometry InSAR combined with change detection for mitigating slope instability, guiding risk management strategies, and integrating geodetic and geotechnical models into mining safety frameworks.

title: Explainable AI in Maze Classification and Segmentation: A Systematic Review
authors: Enugula HariKrishna, Durgesh Nandan
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) has recently been introduced as a paradigm for ensuring transparency, interpretability and increasing trust in complex ML systems. In a segmental or classification context in the maze area, XAI allows for an insight on model behavior, supporting the validation of the algorithm and the human understanding of the internal decision process. In this systematic review, we summarize the existing work on explainable approaches to computational tasks related to mazes, considering approaches based on feature attribution, rule-based explanation, visualization, and model simplification. Moreover, the review explains how models can be re-trained with XAI techniques, how XAI can be adapted to segmentation problems and how interpretability can improve robustness and generalization. Furthermore, we classify studies according to methodology, datasets, and evaluation metrics and determine main trends, strengths, and weaknesses across the literature. Finally, a discussion highlights research gaps and directions for future research that can improve the reliability and interpretability of machine learning-based maze classification and segmentation.

title: DRaGon: Robust Emitter Geolocation via Deep Learning with NTP-Synchronized RTL-SDR Receivers
authors: Zulhaidir Hamid, Elyas Palantei, Intan Sari Areni
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Accurate radio frequency (RF) geolocation is vital for spectrum management, public safety, and defense. The Time Difference of Arrival (TDoA) method is effective but requires sub-nanosecond synchronization, traditionally achievable only with costly GPS-disciplined hardware. In contrast, the Network Time Protocol (NTP) over public networks have millisecond-level jitter, making it not suitable for conventional TDoA. This paper introduces DRaGon (Distributed Radio Geolocation), a deep learning framework that bypasses explicit time-difference estimation by learning an end-to-end mapping from raw in-phase and quadrature (I/Q) samples to geographic coordinates. Using three low-cost RTL-SDR receivers synchronized solely by NTP over cellular networks, we trained and evaluated DRaGon on a real-world dataset of more than $\mathbf{1 9 8, 0 0 0 ~ F M}$ broadcast samples collected in Semarang, Indonesia. Results show that DRaGon achieves a mean absolute localization error of 426 meters, a 63.24% improvement over sophisticated RSSI-based trilateration baselines. These findings challenge the assumption that precise hardware synchronization is indispensable for TDoA and demonstrate that synchronization errors and multipath effects can be treated as part of a learnable $\mathbf{R F}$ fingerprint, enabling affordable and scalable RF geolocation systems.

title: Land Suitability Mapping for Agrivoltaic System in Agusan Del Sur Using Geographic Information System (GIS) and Analytic Hierarchy Process
authors: Julia Marie A. Condolon, Richelle Mae U. Intic, Maria Belinda D. Campana, Arnaldo C. Gagula
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Energy transition is crucial for long-term growth and climate resilience, offering sustainable development solutions. As global energy demand increases due to population growth and industrial intensification, addressing climate change requires shifting from fossil fuels to renewables. However, largescale solar installations require extensive land allocation, posing challenges for agricultural production. The concept of Agrivoltaics, combining solar energy production and agriculture on the same land, has emerged to address this issue. This study focuses on identifying suitable areas for implementing Agrivoltaic systems in Agusan del Sur, a province in the Philippines. Geographic Information System (GIS) techniques and the Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) approach are utilized to assess land suitability based on various criteria, including solar radiation, slope, land cover, aspect, elevation, soil type, and distance to roads and protected areas, which serve as exclusion factors. Results show that the suitability of agrivoltaic installations is highly dependent on Solar Radiation availability, followed by slope of the area, Land Cover, Aspect, Elevation, Soil Type, and Proximity to Roads. The results also provide a weighted overlay suitability map, highlighting the most suitable areas for Agrivoltaic Systems installation, which are the municipalities of Prosperidad, San Francisco, and Rosario in the province of Agusan del Sur.

title: Comparative Analysis of Few-Shot Learning and Transfer Learning for Medical Imaging Tumour Classification
authors: Sheenam Middha, Abhay Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Processing and Electronics Design (ICSPED)
abstract: Prostate cancer is the second most common disease in men in the United States and touches the lives of millions of men every year, thereby becoming an important global health issue. The glandular prostate (PG), which plays a pivotal role in seminal fluid production, is the main organ that gets targeted by this disease. Since magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is not invasive and gives accurate soft-tissue contrast, it has become an essential diagnostic tool for prostate cancer. To enhance the accuracy and efficacy of prostate cancer detection from MRI images, we present in this paper an improved deep learning approach that employs the InceptionResNetV2 model and exploits transfer learning. Compared with traditional diagnosis approaches, our model is effective in extracting intricate features and enhancing sensitivity. We also explore the viability of few-shot learning in medical imaging using prototypical networks with a minor portion of the dataset and reveal the potential of few-shot learning strategies in cases with limited annotated medical images and lay the groundwork for future studies. Large-scale validation shows strong and stable results, endorsing the applicability of our method in clinical environments for early detection and tailored treatment planning, eventually leading to enhanced patient outcomes.

title: Parameters Identification of MESFET by Including Thermal Effect, Surface and Substrate Trappings Using Modified Hippopotamus Optimization
authors: S. Thulasi Prasad, M Madhusudhan Reddy, Satti Sudha Mohan Reddy, Shuvanka Maji, Smruti Ranjan Nayak, G.Simi Margarat, M Naresh Kumar, Ajay Sudhir Bale, Kandi Bhanu Prakash
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: Metal Semiconductor Field Effect Transistors (MESFETs) configured in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 H}$</tex>-SiC are utilized in various applications that impact our daily lives. The analysis of MESFET models under direct current characteristics is quite complex. This paper considers three main effects: surface trapping, thermal effects, and substrate trapping, to elucidate the direct current characteristics of the MESFET. The suggested model for the current-voltage (I-V) curves incorporates the Caughey-Thomas configuration. This configuration takes into account the trapping of electrons in the substrate through various deep-level trapping methods. Additionally, it addresses the field-dependent mobility of electrons and provides an analysis of the two-dimensional distribution of charge located beneath the gate. This comprehensive approach allows for a better understanding of the electronic behavior in the device under different operating conditions. This proposed method is comprehensive, accounting for all significant thermal effects and material flaws, including traps. Nonetheless, solving intricate mathematical equations of the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 H}$</tex>-SiC MESFET to derive precise I-V characteristics across various regions is quite challenging due to its complexity. Consequently, the Hippopotamus Optimization Method (HOM) is employed to address a range of intricate equations related to 4H-SiC MESFET, thereby accurately deriving the I-V characteristics. Consequently, the performance of the suggested analytical model closely resembles that of a real-time <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 H - S i C}$</tex> MESFET. Various behavioral characteristics are obtained and evaluated using the MATLAB programming software.

title: FPGA Stereo Visual Slam with Efficient Stereo Feature Matching and Key-Frame Generation
authors: Miyuru Thathsara, Damith Anhettigama, Siew-Kei Lam
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Field-Programmable Logic and Applications (FPL)
abstract: Stereo visual Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) enables autonomous agents to navigate complex environments by simultaneously building metric maps and localizing within them. In this paper, we present the first FPGA-based System-on-Chip (SoC) implementation of stereo visual SLAM. Our design reduces stereo feature matching latency by dynamically grouping descriptors based on localized image regions and descriptor similarity, while preserving matching accuracy. We also revisit conventional key-frame generation strategies and show that some criteria lead to unnecessary map expansion and runtime instability on resource-constrained platforms. To address this, we propose a new key-frame generation method based on the tracking status of local map points in the current frame. Extensive evaluations on standard benchmarks show that our system achieves high robustness and real-time performance with notable performance-power gains, while maintaining accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art stereo SLAM methods on embedded platforms. Real-world experiments further confirm our system's suitability for practical robotic applications.

title: Maximum FPGA: A 32K-Point 32-Parallel Floating Point FFT
authors: Martin Langhammer, Bogdan Pasca
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Field-Programmable Logic and Applications (FPL)
abstract: FPGAs offer a powerful and flexible platform to build complex systems on. But the potential - both in density and clock frequency - is often missed. In this work we present a massively parallel FFT, which can process a 32 K point FFT with 32-parallel IEEE-754 single-precision floatingpoint streams of complex data. Each core uses 1281 DSP Blocks, and can be packed into a near 100 % DSP Block density in an Altera Agilex-7 FPGA. Six such cores have been instantiated in a system, using 90 % of the device capability. In all cases our designs close timing at nearly 770 MHz, which is the restricted frequency of the DSP Block in floatingpoint mode. The contributions of this work are manifold. We demonstrate approaches that can be used to fill even larger FPGAs to high density with high performance. We also introduce optimizations for the FFT that can be used by all types of (including non-FFT) applications, from simple implementations to the very large parallel examples we use to demonstrate our research.

title: OPTISCHED: A Cognitive Algorithm for Streamlined IoT Task Scheduling in Cloud Computing
authors: Sangeetha K, Kanthimathi M, Anuratha K, Harini T, Hamrithaa Shree A, Kaviya M
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Cloud computing is a most used technology that gives access to the user and manage resources such as storage, data bases and applications over the internet. There are several cloud services for wide variety of uses, so efficient task scheduling in cloud computing is important as it ensures optimised consumption. So, we propose a solution for optimised power consumption by reducing overall makespan. We have taken up overall makespan as well as power consumption as our proposed algorithm’s [Optisched] performance metrics. This paper works on the comparison of proposed scheduling algorithm, i.e Optisched with other existing algorithms to overcome the drawbacks of these scheduling algorithm by certain percentage. When the Virtual machines are increased while tasks being assigned through Optisched, the overall makespan is reduced. Due to the increase in virtual machine along with our optisched algorithm, overall execution time is reduced by completely avoiding idle time in the virtual machines through streamlined task scheduling as of which energy wastage is also eliminated. Through this, we can achieve a streamlined usage of IoT Task scheduling.

title: Versatile Place and Route with Continuous Routing Runtime Prediction and Smart Route Termination
authors: Andrew David Gunter, Steven J E Wilton
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Field-Programmable Logic and Applications (FPL)
abstract: Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) routing is computationally expensive, taking hours or days with no guarantee of success. While prior work has used machine learning (ML) to guide placement and routing or predict routing outcomes, the process remains challenging to model precisely. This demo presents a new machine learning-based tool that addresses this problem.

title: A Bayesian Multiobject Tracking Approach with Neural-Enhanced Motion and Measurement Models
authors: Shaoxiu Wei, Mingchao Liang, Florian Meyer
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: This paper introduces a hybrid Bayesian multiobject tracking (MOT) method that relies on a statistical model and makes use of neural networks to enhance parts of the model that have been identified as overly simplistic. Contrary to previous work, neural enhancement is performed for both the measurement and the motion model. To ensure tractable computation, our framework uses belief propagation (BP) to avoid high-dimensional operations combined with sequential Monte Carlo methods to perform low-dimensional operations efficiently. The resulting MOT method combines the flexibility and robustness of model-based approaches with the capability to learn from data of neural networks. We evaluate the performance of the proposed method based on the nuScenes autonomous driving dataset and demonstrate that it has state-of-the-art performance.

title: Coarray DOA Estimation with Sparse Arrays Using Variable Window Size Spatial Smoothing
authors: Wesley S. Leite, Rodrigo C. de Lamare, Yuriy Zakharov, Wei Liu, Martin Haardt
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: In this work, we introduce a variable window size (VWS) spatial smoothing framework that enhances coarray-based direction of arrival (DOA) estimation for sparse linear arrays. By compressing the smoothing aperture, the proposed VWS Coarray MUSIC (VWS-CA-MUSIC) and VWS Coarray root-MUSIC (VWS-CA-rMUSIC) algorithms replace part of the perturbed rank-one outer products in the smoothed coarray data with unperturbed low-rank additional terms, increasing the separation between signal and noise subspaces, while preserving the signal subspace span. We also derive the bounds that guarantees identifiability, by limiting the values that can be assumed by the compression parameter. Simulations with sparse geometries reveal significant performance improvements and complexity savings relative to the fixed-window coarray MUSIC method.

title: How to and Who Should Read This Book
authors: N/A
abstract: None

title: Basics of Category Theory
authors: N/A

title: An Integrative First-Year Induction Program for Computing Students: Design and Evaluation
authors: Toni Moilanen, Ville Isomöttönen, Paavo Nieminen, Tiina Koskelainen

title: Adaptive Store-and-Recall Memory for Domain Continual Learning
authors: Shutian Zhang, Changsheng Li, Guoren Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Domain continual learning requires overcoming catastrophic forgetting while training in sequential domains. A common solution is to store and replay samples from previous domains to preserve knowledge, but such replay-based strategies raise privacy and storage concerns and are often impractical in real-world settings. In this paper, we propose a rehearsal-free framework, which leverages a store-and-recall strategy to generate a compatible optimization subspace, ensuring effective transfer for preventing catastrophic forgetting. To establish a foundation for effectively leveraging historical knowledge, we introduce a Frequency-Aware Filtering, which extracts key representations in the frequency domain and compacts them into internalmemory to support rehearsal-free retention. To further exploit historical information effectively, we design a Gain-Adaptive Gating mechanism integrated with a Knowledge Fusion module, which selectively activates historical knowledge based on gain signals to avoid negative transfer caused by distribution shifts. Extensive experiments show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art continual learning methods across standard benchmarks under the domain-incremental setting.

title: Deep Semantic Matching Based on Selective Feature Attention and Divide-and-Conquer Strategy
authors: Yan Ren, Chaocheng Zhang, Baokang Li
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Traditional semantic matching models in question answering (QA) and dialogue tasks often process all tokens uniformly, overlooking the varying granularity required for matching key components in user queries — such as actions, entities, and events. This leads to insufficient recognition of critical semantic information and abstract intents, thereby compromising matching accuracy and user experience. To address this issue, this paper proposes a deep semantic matching method based on selective feature attention and a divide-and-conquer strategy. Specifically, the model employs a keyword-intent separation mechanism to perform fine-grained matching for keywords and coarsegrained matching for intents. Additionally, a selective feature attention mechanism dynamically adjusts the importance of semantic features, enhancing the model's ability to capture essential semantics. Without incurring additional inference costs, the proposed method achieves robust and effective performance improvements across multiple benchmark datasets. This is because our keyword - intent separation and selective attention modules are applied only during training, and the inference stage uses exactly the same encoder and classifier architecture as the baseline model. The results demonstrate that the approach provides strong support for QA matching tasks.

title: Integrated Feature Extraction Using Principal Components and Deep Autoencoder With Extra Trees for Intrusion Detection Systems
authors: Kumari K, Uma Mageswari, Khushbu Pandey, A. Pushpalatha, Girish Kalele, Balamurugan V
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: The study suggests a modern Intrusion Detection System (IDS) that uses PCA (Principal Component Analysis) and an UDAE (Unsupervised Deep Autoencoder) for good feature extraction, then uses the Extra Tree (ET) classification method. It tackles the typical issues of working with many attributes and an uneven distribution of labels in network flow datasets. At the start, data is balanced and SMOTE-ENN is used to help minority classes be better represented. PCA separates data with strong linear relationships and only keeps strong and moderate main components in the analysis. These elements go through UDAE which looks for non-linear patterns and makes sure the output feature set is as reduced as possible. All the features are then labeled by using machine learning models and ET shows the highest accuracy. The proposed PC-UDAE framework was tested against popular benchmark sets and showed better accuracy, F1-score and faster speed than previous methods. Research demonstrates that the model outperforms other approaches which makes it a solid and dependable solution for detecting many kinds of cyberattacks.

title: Quantum Machine Learning for Disease Prediction: A Comparative and Multimodal Study on Early-Stage and Chronic Conditions
authors: Nitika Bahl, Pranjal Singh, Kulvinder Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Quantum Machine Learning (QML) is fast becoming a powerful instrument in medical diagnostics because it can process high-dimensional data and small samples efficiently. This paper is a detailed analysis of QML models used for disease prediction, with a relative comparison of Variational Quantum Classifiers (VQC), Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNN), and Quantum Support Vector Machines (QSVM) in clinical environments. We suggest a new multimodal quantumclassical hybrid approach that analyzes electronic health records (EHR), medical imaging, and biosignal data by means of dedicated QML pipelines with late fusion for ultimate decisionmaking. Recent IEEE literature is integrated in experimental performance glimpses to demonstrate the merits, difficulties, and viability of QML in real-world healthcare applications. The envisioned architecture provides the foundation for future applications of quantum-enhanced diagnostics with existing quantum simulators as well as emerging hardware.

title: A Hybrid Approach to Classify Sentences for Instructional Content Identification in Sinhala
authors: S.A.R. Nethmini, Lochandaka Ranathunga
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid sentence classification framework for instructional content in the Sinhala language. It combines rule-based categorization, using domain-adapted bags of words and linguistic features, with a machine learning approach using sentence embeddings and logistic regression. The system efficiently distinguishes and filters out non-instructional sentences such as introductions, engagement phrases, and tips, while reliably identifying instructional steps. Experimental evaluation demonstrates the effectiveness of this strategy in accurately identifying and filtering instructional sentences in Sinhala text, forming a foundation for future applications in automated content organization and analysis. The approach was evaluated against instructional video-based content and achieved about 95.34% performance accuracy.

title: SmartSphere: Bridging AI-Powered Automation and Tesla Coil Wireless Energy for Smarter Living
authors: Jerun Kingston Jurison Jenaraj, Ayushyaa Umasuthan, Vithiyasahar Vigneswaran, Kithusshand Raveendran, Kanagasabai Thiruthanigesan, Sanvitha Kasthuriarachchi
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: This paper presents SmartSphere, an intelligent, secure, and wire-free home automation system that synergizes artificial intelligence, multi-factor access control, and resonant wireless power transmission. Built on an edge-computing architecture using ESP32 and Arduino Mega 2560 microcontrollers, SmartSphere integrates facial recognition, fingerprint authentication (96.7% accuracy), and IR-based presence detection to reduce false activations by 75%. The system employs OpenCV and TensorFlow Lite for real-time anomaly detection and environmental personalization, including weather-responsive adjustments and emotion-aware lighting via facial expression analysis. A key innovation is the incorporation of a Tesla coil-based wireless power transmission module, which eliminates conventional wiring constraints and reduces installation cabling by 86%. Experimental validation demonstrates a 14% improvement in energy efficiency, 75% faster response time (0.3 s), and seamless compatibility with 92% of tested IoT devices. Through comprehensive testing and validation, this research establishes SmartSphere as a secure, intelligent, and sustainable solution for next-generation smart homes, addressing the limitations of traditional wired systems while enhancing the user experience through AI-driven personalization.

title: Designing Emotionally Intelligent Embodied Agents for Immersive Virtual Reality Experiences
authors: Seyedbehnam Rad, Razeen Hussain, Manuela Chessa, Fabio Solari
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and eXtended and Virtual Reality (AIxVR)
abstract: The integration of conversational agents in virtual reality (VR) offers promising opportunities for applications such as training, education, and mental health support. However, most current VR agents lack empathic capabilities, which are crucial for fostering engagement, trust, and effective interaction. To address this gap, we present EMBRACE, a modular framework that combines natural language processing, emotion inference, expressive text-to-speech synthesis, and AI-driven facial animation to deliver emotionally responsive VR agents. We implemented EMBRACE within a counselling-inspired virtual environment and conducted a user study, comparing empathic and nonempathic agent conditions. Subjective measures included usability, presence, and agent evaluation, while objective measures incorporated heart rate and heart rate variability. Results show that empathic behaviours enhanced spatial presence and revealed trends toward higher general presence and physiological engagement. Additional analyses indicated that prior chatbot experience shaped usability and agent ratings, while gender influenced certain presence dimensions. Our findings demonstrate both the feasibility and benefits of implementing empathic VR agents.

title: ZoneFit: Enabling Zoned Flash Benefits via Granularity-Matched SLC Management
authors: Dingcui Yu, Zonghuan Yan, Yunpeng Song, Yanyun Wang, Xinghui Duan, Congming Gao, Yina Lv, Liang Shi
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th Non-Volatile Memory Systems and Applications Symposium (NVMSA)
abstract: Quad-Level Cell (QLC) flash technology is increasingly adopted in consumer-grade storage systems due to its high density and cost efficiency. However, its inherent two-step programming process and lack of power-loss protection necessitate routing writes through a Single-Level Cell (SLC) cache to ensure data integrity. Under the Zoned UFS protocol, the SLC cache suffers from mismatched management granularity, leading to an enlarged mapping table, inefficient space reclamation, and migration conflicts. To address these issues, we propose ZoneFit, a zoned SLC–QLC co-management framework that incorporates zone-level SLC capacity allocation and zone-level SLC migration. Evaluations on a consumer-grade zoned storage emulator demonstrate that ZoneFit significantly enhances read performance and prolongs the lifespan of flash storage.

title: The Evolution of Digital Cultural Heritage Towards Human-Centered Storyliving Virtual Worlds
authors: Eleftherios Anastasovitis, Georgia Georgiou, Spiros Nikolopoulos, Ioannis Kompatsiaris
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and eXtended and Virtual Reality (AIxVR)
abstract: In the last fifteen years the evolution of immersive and emerging technologies had a high impact in creative and cultural heritage sectors. Initially, 3D graphics applications evolved into experiences of varying degrees of immersion, depending on the type of extended reality used. The evolution of graphics and computer technology shapes the digital transformation of cultural heritage experiences. This study offers, through four indicative use cases of virtual reality applications, the evolution of digital culture over the last decade. Starting from the 3D application of the virtual anthropology museum, it moves on to the highly immersive experiences of the Scladina cave and the immersive experience of the Virtual Palace of Aigai, supported by enhanced gamification. The research culminates in the innovative storyliving virtual museum EPANASTASIS-1821 and its initial evaluation. At the same time, it highlights future trends in the transformation of digital culture in the light of virtual worlds.

title: Adaptive Robust Control for Underactuated Bipedal Parallel Wheel-Legged Robots: A Nash Game-Based Constraint Following Approach
authors: Huayong Zhong, Hao Zhang, Shengchao Zhen, Hao Sun, Xiaolong Chen, Xiaoli Liu, Ye-Hwa Chen
abstract: This article proposes a Nash game-optimized adaptive robust control framework for bipedal parallel wheel-legged robots. Specifically, the framework targets a balance between tracking accuracy and control effort under underactuation, constraint coupling, and substantial uncertainties. To address these challenges, a constraint following adaptive robust controller is adopted, while gain selection is posed as a two-player Nash game between performance and cost objectives. Consequently, the resulting equilibrium yields controller gains without heuristic tuning. Furthermore, Lyapunov analysis establishes uniform ultimate boundedness of the closed-loop trajectories under bounded disturbances and model errors. Finally, numerical simulations verify that the proposed approach achieves a superior balance, concurrently enhancing tracking accuracy while significantly reducing control effort compared to conventional methods.

title: Hierarchical Task-Building-Based Multisource Adaptive Meta Transfer Learning for Cross-Domain Fault Diagnosis
authors: Weichao Yue, Haiyang Hu, Fang Wang, Xiaoxue Wan, Xiaofang Chen
abstract: Cross-domain fault diagnosis leverages knowledge from multiple source domains to improve diagnostic accuracy in target domain. However, existing methods align source and target domains either jointly or independently, often neglecting the distributional discrepancies among source domains, which hinders effective knowledge transfer. To address this issue, we proposes a hierarchical task-building-based multisource adaptive meta transfer learning (HTB-MSAMTL) framework. First, the semantic alignment bidirectional embedding module pretrains meta-learning parameters through label-feature embedding alignment. Second, a task split strategy is designed for hierarchical meta-tasks, creating multiple domain pairs for learning long-term embeddable high-level meta-knowledge. Finally, a prototype feature reprojection network is developed to optimize calibrated prototypes and minimizes cross-domain distribution discrepancies through a differentiable closed-form solver. Moreover, learnable matrices replace fixed prototypes to enable adaptively tuned prototype representations. HTB-MSAMTL is evaluated on Tennessee Eastman Process, Case Western Reserve University, and Aluminum Electrolysis Process datasets. Results demonstrate HTB-MSAMTL outperforms existing methods in multisource cross-domain fault diagnosis under scarce labeled data scenarios.

title: On broadband non-synchronous measurements beamforming: A Laplacian-enhanced low-rank tensor completion approach
authors: Long Chen, Jiaxin Hu, Xiaoang Liu, Weize Sun, Lei Huang
publish: IEEE Journal on Miniaturization for Air and Space Systems
abstract: Constrained by the physical geometry of microphone arrays, acoustic beamforming faces limitations in its operational frequency range and scanning scale. Non-synchronous measurements (NSM) offer a viable solution by moving an array to construct a virtual large dense array. Our team, in a previous study , proposed a tensor completion method based on the Tensor Nuclear Norm (TNN), which addresses the frequency selection problem by reconstructing the Cross-Spectral Tensor (CST) of broadband signals . However, the TNN , as a convex surrogate for tensor rank, has a limitation: it assigns equal weight to all singular values, which restricts its performance under low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions. As an extension and improvement of that work , this paper proposes a novel approach: a non-synchronous measurements method for broadband multiple-sound-source localization based on Laplacian-enhanced low-rank tensor completion. This method utilizes the Laplace function as a superior non-convex surrogate, enabling it to automatically assign weights based on singular value importance, thereby achieving a more robust recovery of the low-rank tensor structure. Simulations and experimental studies demonstrate that, compared to TNN and other methods, the proposed L-TNN algorithm produces sound maps with lower side-lobes and higher accuracy, especially under challenging conditions of low SNR and sparse measurements.

title: Unpaired Image Translation Using Cycle-Consistent GANs for the Development of a Digital Artwork Restoration System
authors: Adesh Kumar Mishra, S. Goshteeswaran, Aashim Dhawan, S Gowri, Naveen N M, Neha Jaswani
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: The paper suggests an effective strategy of digital art restoration grounded on unpaired image-to-image translation through CycleGANs (Cycle-Consistent Generative Adversarial Networks). The model uses a two-GAN based mapping between the damaged and restored artworks without using paired images dataset. To improve the semantic reconstruction, the generator is brought up with two attention mechanisms, namely, spatial attention and channel attention. Training: new loss functions: smooth L1-based perceptual cycle consistency loss, identity loss, and SSIM loss, using VGG-19 feature embeddings as optimisation target. Two datasets of 1349, 2041 artwork images were experimentally evaluated. The model presented in this paper has gained the best restoration results with MSE of 68.99, SSIM of 0.69 and PSNR of 30.88 on the 'Art images' dataset compared to CycleGAN (MSE: 77.11, SSIM: 0.60, PSNR: 29.54) and Pix2Pix. Also, the model achieved MSE: 104.91, SSIM: 0.54, PSNR: 27.94 on the 'Best artwork of all time information set. Visual and quantitative comparisons affirm that much better details preservation and textures restoration are achieved. The method proposed shall further automated restoration of artwork using context aware learning in GANs.

title: Deep Convolutional Neural Network-Based Human Bone Classification with Multi-View Medical Image Transformation
authors: Markala Karthik, N Prabhakaran, Trapty Agarwal, R. Nallakumar, Nimesh Raj, Mary Gladence L
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: Medical imaging of human bones classification is an important part of the diagnosis and treatment plan. In this study, a DCNN (Deep Convolutional Neural Network) method of automatically classifying human bones with the use of multiview medical image transformations is presented. With the help of Musculoskeletal Radiographs (MURA) dataset containing 40,005 X-ray images of seven different categories, the proposed model is trained on 36,808 images and tested on 3,197 images. The architecture consists of four convolutional layers, four pooling layers, one flattened layer and two dense layers with a dropout rate of 0.2 to avoid overfitting. The experimental findings also indicate that the DCNN converges at an accuracy of 96.37 percent and precision of 95.45 percent, implying its strength and predictive performance. The method shows a great promise in aiding radiologists in the classification of bones in the correct manner, improving the speed and accuracy of the clinical workflow.

title: Automated Detection of Middle Ear Infections Using Squeeze-and-Excitation ConvNeXt
authors: M. Rethina Kumari, V. Anusuya
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Children and adults are prone to ear infections, especially otitis media, which cause serious problems if left untreated. Traditional diagnostic approaches rely mainly on visual examinations, which is subjective and variable amongst practitioners. A computer-aided detection system that uses an effective image processing and Deep Learning (DL) architecture to automate the identification of ear infections has been proposed as a solution to this problem. Hence, this works deploys an enhanced Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) - ConvNeXt model proposed for automated detection of middle ear infections. The proposed approach begins with Bilateral Filter in preprocessing stage to eliminate noise while keeping edge structures that are critical for accurate diagnosis. In order to improve the separation of healthy and sick areas in otoscopic images, cluster pixel regions are then subjected to Gaussian Kernel Mean Shift Segmentation. Boundary Descriptors are retrieved to emphasize the infection zones' form and edge-based characteristics for effective depiction. It provides compact and discriminative shape-based characteristics, are then used to capture the boundary features of segmented regions. These characteristics are then classified using enhanced SE ConvNeXt model, which combines the capabilities of ConvNeXt architecture with SE attention mechanism to improve feature selectivity and diagnostic accuracy. Experimental assessments using Python software show that this framework achieves good accuracy of 99.97%, precision of 96.9%, and recall of 96.9% in detecting middle ear infections. With its ability to diagnose earrelated problems more quickly, accurately, and non-invasively, the proposed approach shows possibilities for inclusion into clinical decision support systems.

title: A Reinforcement Learning-Adapter Renewable Energy Forecasting Framework for Two-Stage Distribution Network Scheduling
authors: Chenghan Li, Yinliang Xu, Hongbin Sun
abstract: With deepening renewable penetration, distribution-network operators must rely on high-quality forecasts to schedule distributed energy resources. However, most existing Prediction– then–Optimization (PTO) approaches rely solely on forecasted values as inputs, while overlooking the significant impact of the coupling between actual and forecasted values on operational costs. This limitation hinders the practicality and deployment of many decision-oriented methods in power systems. To address this issue, this paper investigates the two-stage scheduling problem of distribution networks and formulates it as a Coupled Prediction–then–Optimization (CPTO) problem. Then, we propose a RL-driven decision-focused framework that integrates a Generative Pretrained Transformer (GPT)-based forecasting module with an improved Actor–Critic Reinforcement Learning (RL) adapter. Specifically, a GPT-based forecasting model with task-adaptive loss is proposed to capture the temporal characteristics of renewable energy outputs. On this basis, an RL adapter is designed to learn a decision-focused policy that dynamically refines the base forecasts, thereby effectively reducing the actual operating cost of the distribution network. Case studies on the IEEE 33-bus, IEEE 141-bus and IEEE 570-bus systems demonstrate that, compared with best baselines, the proposed framework reduces operational costs by 2.20% , 2.28% and 1.80% respectively, thereby validating its effectiveness and superiority in terms of both economic efficiency and operational reliability.

title: Multi-Granularity Token Learning for Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation
authors: Muxin Liao, Yingqiong Peng, Yuting Sun, Wenju Huang, Yinglong Wang, Kun Zhan, Yuhang Zhang
abstract: Recent Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation (DGSS) methods have harnessed vision foundation models (VFMs) to achieve remarkable performance in unseen domains. Two key issues require immediate attention from these methods. First, these methods ignore the learning of contextual information, since VFMs are always pretrained on the image classification task, which means that the frozen backbone of these VFMs struggles to extract contextual information used for dense prediction tasks. Second, these methods focus on enhancing domain-invariant information within the learnable tokens or the features extracted by the frozen backbone (e.g., normalizing their style variances), inevitably losing the effective information. To address these two key issues, this paper proposes a Multi-Granularity Token Learning (MGTL) approach to fine-tune VFMs for DGSS, which uses the visual and text embeddings from the CLIP model for optimizing a set of learnable tokens to learn the domain-invariant information of two granularities, including the domain-invariant contextual and conceptual information. Specifically, first, a contextual relation learning strategy is proposed to assist tokens in capturing and embedding contextual information into VFMs, which is crucial to the semantic segmentation task. Second, a conceptual learning strategy is proposed to acquire instance-level conceptual information for the set of learnable tokens and embed it into VFMs to enhance instance-level domain-invariant information. The proposed MGTL approach is evaluated on multiple DGSS benchmarks, achieving superior performance to recent DGSS methods while maintaining low computational cost in trainable parameters during the fine-tuning stage.

title: Cross-Modal 3D Gaussian Reconstruction: Fusing Image, Geometry, and Open-Vocabulary Semantics for Large-Scale Real-World Scenes
authors: Yuchen Li, Jing Li, Kai Qin, Haitao Wu, Yin Gao, Banghui Yang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3D GS) has revolutionized scene representation, efficient photorealistic rendering, and novel view synthesis. However, when applied to complex large-scale real-world scenes, 3D GS faces two major challenges which are geometric errors and rendering artifacts. To address these challenges, we propose FuseGS, a novel cross-modal Gaussian reconstruction framework that seamlessly fuses multi-view images and dense LiDAR point clouds, along with open-vocabulary semantics from large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to achieve more accurate global geometry and realistic renderings than the existing methods. FuseGS introduces three key innovations. First, to overcome low geometric accuracy in image-only methods, we propose a novel LiDAR-guided Gaussian initialization method to ensure strong geometric supervision. Second, to deal with the difficulty of integrating 2D VLMs semantics into 3D space, we propose a novel category-specific dual-cue refinement method to improve semantics and geometry alignment. Third, to remove rendering artifacts caused by dynamic objects, we introduce a novel semantic-based adaptive density control method to effectively maintain static background reconstruction. To demonstrate the robustness of our framework, we evaluate FuseGS using both self-collected data from handheld and Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) platforms, as well as public datasets. Compared to the baseline 3D GS method, FuseGS unifies joint optimization of photometric, geometric and static-aware semantics, resulting in 50% improvement in geometric accuracy and 4.8 dB improvement in rendering quality. The method achieves 67.43% mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) without manual annotation. Ultimately, FuseGS has demonstrated excellent potential for Earth observation applications such as smart cities and autonomous driving.

title: AutoScaleAI: An AI-Powered Framework for Cloud Resource Optimization Using Telemetry Analytics
authors: Faiz Gouri, Pavan Kota, Mahesh Shivnatri, Kuber Jain
abstract: Cloud infrastructure operators face increasing challenges in balancing performance, cost, and reliability due to dynamicworkloads and complex interdependency. This paper presentsAutoScaleAI, an AI-driven telemetry analytics framework designed to proactively optimize cloud resource utilization and reduce operational costs.The framework integrates a hybrid prediction model Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM) and Long Short-TermMemory (LSTM) networks, used to forecast short-horizon CPU, memory, and network utilization trends, enabling proactive scaling decisions. It further incorporates a two-tier anomaly detection engine Isolation Forest (IF) and Variational Autoencoders (VAE) to identify sudden workload surges, latency deviations, and telemetry drift that may violate service level objectives. Telemetry data modeled on real-world Azure deployments (synthetic simulations validated against historical traces) are used to train and evaluate the system across compute, storage, and network dimensions. In addition, we conduct a controlled real-world pilot on Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) with live telemetry and production APIs, validating end-to-end feasibility and quantifying operational overhead. Experimental results show that AutoScaleAI achieves up to 38% cost reduction and a 35% increase in resource efficiency, outperforming traditional threshold-based and deep reinforcement learning baselines. Additionally, the system achieves a decision latency of 235 milliseconds, making it suitable for real-time cloud optimization scenarios. AutoScaleAI is further validated through real-world case studies in e-commerce, finance, and healthcare environments. This work contributes a scalable and adaptive solution for AI-powered cloud management, offering practical benefits for both hyperscale providers and enterprise consumers.

title: Fact-Checking With Contextual Narratives: Leveraging Retrieval-Augmented LLMs for Social Media Analysis
authors: Arka Ujjal Dey, Muhammad Junaid Awan, Georgia Channing, Christian Schroeder de Witt, John Collomosse
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Fact-checking systems have gained traction as scalable solutions, yet they often face challenges such as handling diverse evidence sources, integrating multimodal data, and presenting comprehensive narratives. In this work, we propose cluster-based retrieval augmented verification with explanation (CRAVE), a novel framework that integrates retrieval-augmented large language models (LLMs) with clustering techniques to address multimodal misinformation on social media. The framework is designed to process multimodal inputs (text and images) and iteratively refine evidence through agent-based mechanisms. We validated the framework on multiple real-world and synthetic datasets, showing that breaking up evidence into narrative clusters improves both retrieval precision, clustering quality, and judgment accuracy, showcasing its potential as a robust decision-support tool for fact-checkers.

title: Unified Functional Spectrum Analysis: A Framework for Understanding Nonverbal Multimodal Multifunctional Interactions in Multiparty Conversations
authors: Momoka Tajima, Issa Tamura, Ayaka Sasaki, Kazuhiro Otsuka
abstract: An analytic framework called unified functional spectrum analysis (U-FSA) to reveal the multimodal multifunctional interplay that emerges and comprises interpersonal interactions in multiparty conversations is proposed. U-FSA aims to quantitatively analyze the multimodal nonverbal behaviors of people engaging in conversations to determine how they interact with each other through from a functional perspective . Specifically, U-FSA employs a functional spectrum, which is defined as a vector representing the perceptual intensities of multifunctionalities of nonverbal behaviors. Unlike the previously employed synergistic functional spectrum analysis (sFSA) technique, which focuses solely on individual bimodal behaviors, U-FSA is a unified framework that is able to analyze multiparty, multimodal, multifunctional interactions. This framework incorporates three key extension strategies focusing on multimodal, multiparty, and temporal dimensions. Multimodal extension enables U-FSA to handle as many modalities as necessary by concatenating multimodal functional spectra. The multiparty extension is designed to address grouplevel interactions among participants on the basis of their roles, specifically the speaker, the addressee, and other listeners. The temporal extension addresses the interaction dynamics, including delayed reactions among interlocutors. These strategies lead to the generation of group-level multimodal functional spectra, and semiorthogonal nonnegative matrix factorization (SO-NMF) is employed to decompose the spectra into the product of two matrices: the basis matrix, which is called the unified functional basis, and the coefficient matrix, which is called the unified functional spectrum. The former represents the most frequently observed and distinctive patterns within multimodal interactions. The latter indicates how these multimodal interactions emerge and evolve over time. In experiments, this study focuses on head movements, facial expressions, and gaze behaviors during conversations involving four parties, and the results reveals key interaction patterns, including addressing by the speaker using facial expressions and gazes; attentive listening by the addressee and other listeners; the sharing of positive emotions between the speaker and the listeners through gazes; and backchanneling by the addressee and other listeners in response to the speaker’s head movements for turn-taking and rhythm coordination purposes. Additionally, this paper presents convolutional neural networks (CNNs) capable of estimating the unified functional spectrum from observational data, including head poses, facial actions, and gaze directions.

title: Comments and Corrections Corrections to “A Systematic Review on the Development of Transhumeral Prostheses: Structural Design, Control Systems, and Functional Integration”
authors: Paul A. Chasi-Pesántez, Cisne Ortega-Cabrera, Martín León, Jorge O. Ordoñez-Ordoñez, Luis J. Serpa-Andrade, Freddy L. Bueno-Palomeque, Luis F. Guerrero-Vásquez
abstract: Presents corrections to the paper, (A Systematic Review on the Development of Transhumeral Prostheses: Structural Design, Control Systems, and Functional Integration).

title: Attitude Estimated Constraint Circular Motion Control for Underactuated Unicycle Robots_supp1-3677722.mp4
authors: Pushkal Purohit
abstract: This paper investigates the problem of motion confinement for an underactuated unicycle robot while stabilizing it along a desired circular trajectory. It is considered that only the robot's position is available for feedback, and its heading angle measurements are not available. To address this, we design an observer for the robot's position and heading angle estimates using only available position measurements. Leveraging these state estimates and the barrier Lyapunov function (BLF), we propose a variable gain-based control design relying on a composite Lyapunov function that simultaneously captures the desired control objectives and convergence of observer errors. It was shown that the proposed controller not only asymptotically stabilizes the motion of the unicycle robot on the desired circular path but also restricts its trajectories within the predefined circular boundary almost everywhere. Furthermore, we elaborate on how the state constraints imposed in the original problem formulation are effectively enforced through the estimated variables. Both numerical simulations and real-time experiments on the Khepera IV robot are provided.

title: Prompt-Driven Lightweight Foundation Model for Instance Segmentation-Based Fault Detection in Freight Trains
authors: Guodong Sun, Qihang Liang, Xingyu Pan, Moyun Liu, Yang Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Accurate visual fault detection in freight trains remains a critical challenge for intelligent transportation system maintenance, due to complex operational environments, structurally repetitive components, and frequent occlusions or contaminations in safety-critical regions. Conventional instance segmentation methods based on convolutional neural networks and Transformers often suffer from poor generalization and limited boundary accuracy under such conditions. To address these challenges, we propose a lightweight self-prompted instance segmentation framework tailored for freight train fault detection. Our method leverages the Segment Anything Model by introducing a self-prompt generation module that automatically produces task-specific prompts, enabling effective knowledge transfer from foundation models to domain-specific inspection tasks. In addition, we adopt a Tiny Vision Transformer backbone to reduce computational cost, making the framework suitable for real-time deployment on edge devices in railway monitoring systems. We construct a domain-specific dataset collected from real-world freight inspection stations and conduct extensive evaluations. Experimental results show that our method achieves <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$74.6~AP^{\text {box}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$74.2~AP^{\text {mask}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> on the dataset, outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and robustness while maintaining low computational overhead. This work offers a deployable and efficient vision solution for automated freight train inspection, demonstrating the potential of foundation model adaptation in industrial-scale fault diagnosis scenarios. Project page: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/MVME-HBUT/SAM_FTI-FDet</uri>

title: Contact-Aware Morphology Optimization Via Physically Consistent Differentiable Simulation
authors: Filippo Luca Ferretti, Diego Ferigo, Alessandro Croci, Carlotta Sartore, Omar G. Younis, Silvio Traversaro, Daniele Pucci
abstract: Optimizing robot morphology and behavior remains an open challenge, primarily because hardware parameters affect system and contact dynamics, and naive parametrization can yield physically inconsistent designs and unreliable gradients. We propose a simulation-based co-design approach for gradient-based morphology optimization through intermittent contact by expressing inertial quantities, collision geometry, and contact forces as smooth functions of bounded hardware parameters. This parametrization preserves physical consistency across admissible designs and enables trajectory-level differentiation through contact transitions. The methodology is simulator-agnostic and is instantiated in a JAX-based differentiable simulator to leverage accelerator execution and automatic differentiation. We validate the approach by optimizing a single-leg morphology to reach a target jump height under torque limits. Controlled studies further assess correctness: synthetic system identification recovers ground-truth parameters to numerical precision, cross-engine calibration against MuJoCo reaches MSE below <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{-5}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and second-order derivatives match finite differences. Compared to genetic algorithms, gradient-based optimization is substantially faster but more sensitive to initialization, while remaining computationally practical for co-design.

title: On the Importance of Tactile Sensing for Imitation Learning: A Case Study on Robotic Match Lighting_supp2-3677716.pdf
authors: Niklas Funk
abstract: The field of robotic manipulation has advanced significantly in recent years. At the sensing level, several novel tactile sensors have been developed, capable of providing accurate contact information. On a methodological level, learning from demonstrations has proven an efficient paradigm to obtain performant robotic manipulation policies. The combination of both holds the promise to extract crucial contact-related information from the demonstration data and actively exploit it during policy rollouts. However, this integration has so far been underexplored, most notably in dynamic, contact-rich manipulation tasks where precision and reactivity are essential. This work therefore proposes a multimodal, visuotactile imitation learning framework that integrates a modular transformer architecture with a flow-based generative model, enabling efficient learning of fast and dexterous manipulation policies. We evaluate our framework on the dynamic, contact-rich task of robotic match lighting - a task in which tactile feedback influences human manipulation performance. The experimental results highlight the effectiveness of our approach and show that adding tactile information improves policy performance, thereby underlining their combined potential for learning dynamic manipulation from few demonstrations.

title: IIoT Attack Detection Using Genetic Algorithm Optimized Neural Networks
authors: Hamad Alblooshi, Kevser Ovaz Akpinar, Mustafa Akpinar
abstract: The Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) is accelerating digital transformation in industrial sectors through connected sensors, edge devices, and data-driven automation; however, this connectivity also increases exposure to cyber threats that can disrupt safety-critical and mission-critical operations. This study addresses the need for effective IIoT intrusion detection by proposing a hybrid model that combines a genetic algorithm (GA) with a neural network (NN), in which the GA optimizes NN parameters to improve classification performance in complex traffic environments. The proposed approach is evaluated on the Edge-IIoTset dataset, a labeled IIoT intrusion detection benchmark that includes normal traffic and multiple attack families (e.g., DDoS, malware, and injection-related attacks), represented by heterogeneous feature types and characterized by pronounced class imbalance. Dimensionality reduction is applied using principal component analysis to reduce feature redundancy and improve learning efficiency. Experimental results show that the GA-optimized NN achieves up to 95% overall accuracy across the evaluated attack categories, with particularly strong discrimination for dominant attack groups. The findings indicate that evolutionary optimization can enhance the performance of NN-based IDSs and provide a flexible optimization framework for IIoT settings, thereby supporting the development of more resilient intrusion detection solutions for industrial networks.

title: Super-resolution dual-energy CT reconstruction based on a discrete focal spot model
authors: Ping Yang, Jigang Duan, Ligen Shi, Genwei Ma, Xiangang Wang, Wei Zhang
abstract: Objective: The aim of this study was to improve spatial resolution and material decomposition ac curacy in dual-energy computed tomography (DECT) by addressing image degradation caused by focal spot blur ring. Methods: We proposed a weighted super-resolution DECT reconstruction framework based on a discrete focal spot model. A nonlinear imaging model that incorporates focal spot geometry and dual-energy acquisition was constructed by using experimentally measured spot characteristics. On this basis, a high-precision iterative reconstruction method was developed. A novel residual dependent adaptive weighting strategy was introduced to guide the update of material basis images during iterations, thus enabling the method to focus on poorly reconstructed regions and enhancing robustness. Results: Experiments were conducted on both simulated and real DECT datasets with various focal spot sizes and noise levels. The proposed method consistently outperformed conventional re construction methods and achieved superior spatial resolution and improved material decomposition accuracy. Ablation studies further validated the contribution of the adaptive weighting strategy to the reconstruction quality. Conclusions: By modeling focal spot effects explicitly and incorporating an adaptive weighting mechanism, the proposed approach effectively mitigates resolution loss and instability in DECT reconstruction. Significance: This work introduces a generalizable and practical solution for enhancing image quality in DECT, with potential effects on high-precision biomedical imaging and clinical diagnostics.

title: Data Quality in Machine Learning: Boosting the Base for Solid AI Systems
authors: Vidya Sagar S D, Lohith M S, Dileep M R
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Data quality is fundamental to the success of machine learning models. As these models increasingly drive critical decisions across various sectors, ensuring that the underlying data is accurate, consistent, and complete becomes paramount. This paper examines the multiple dimensions of data quality and their impact on machine learning outcomes. We analyse current research and practical scenarios to address common challenges such as noise, missing data, and imbalanced datasets, proposing a systematic approach to assess and enhance data quality. We demonstrate that effective data quality management not only improves model accuracy but also produces more reliable and interpretable results. Through comprehensive data profiling, preprocessing techniques, and continuous monitoring, our approach shows measurable improvements in precision, efficiency, and bias reduction. We provide an extensive literature review with over 20 relevant references and present schematic diagrams that highlight the strategic importance of data quality as a cornerstone of modern AI systems.

title: A Novel MEMS Piezoelectric Microphone for Multiband Acoustic Sensing
authors: Zhuoyue Zheng, Chen Wang, Michael Kraft, Rui P. Martins, Pui-in Mak, Yuan Wang
abstract: This article presents a novel multiband piezoelectric microelectromechanical systems (MEMS) microphone. Equipped with a fully clamped membrane and a closed-loop frequency-tracking system, the proposed microphone achieves acoustic signal detection in both infrasonic and audible frequency bands (with an experimentally verified bandwidth of 0.1 Hz–3.8 kHz). Key innovations include: 1) concurrent infrasound and audio-band operation via a single structural design; 2) piezoelectric actuation-enabled mechanical modulation of acoustic signals to support frequency and amplitude modulation (FM/AM) readout in the low-frequency regime; 3) an optimized electrode pattern tailored for low frequencies to enhance FM/AM sensitivities; and 4) cost-effective performance realized using standard piezoelectric MEMS processes. Experimentally, for audible detection, the microphone attains a baseline sensitivity of −52 dB (re 1 V/Pa) and a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of 61.1 dB. For low-frequency detection, it exhibits an FM sensitivity of 109.3 mHz/Pa and an AM sensitivity of 51.1 <inline-formula content-type="math/tex" xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX" version="MathJax">$\boldsymbol{\mu}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>V/Pa, achieving 2.7<inline-formula content-type="math/tex" xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX" version="MathJax">$\boldsymbol{\times}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and 2.2<inline-formula content-type="math/tex" xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX" version="MathJax">$\boldsymbol{\times}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> SNR improvements over the unmodulated signal at 20 Hz. With customized configurations, the FM and AM sensitivities are further enhanced to 1198.9 mHz/Pa and 457.6 <inline-formula content-type="math/tex" xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX" version="MathJax">$\boldsymbol{\mu}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>V/Pa, respectively. The FM enhancement stems from reallocating the original sensing electrode to drive the membrane on the same device, while the AM improvement relies on the revised electrode pattern. These findings demonstrate the device’s great potential as a solution for wearable devices targeting both voice capture and biometric monitoring via subsonic and low-frequency signatures, including heart rate, respiration, and facial movements.

title: A Position- and Scale-Aware Object-Agent Classifier via Deep Reinforcement Learning for Remote Sensing Imagery
authors: Xian Xu, Yinhe Liu, Yingxin Wu, Yanfei Zhong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Deep object classification is an effective method for land-cover classification in remote sensing images, requiring fewer samples and achieving clearer classification boundaries. However, determining the size and position of the image patches is crucial for accurate deep object classification, and this is often reliant on expert knowledge and trial and error, which limits its general applicability. In this article, a position- and scale-aware object-agent classifier for remote sensing imagery—named ObjectAgents—is proposed. Unlike prior pixel-level or global DRL approaches, this work pioneers an object-centric decision-making framework. The task of selecting representative image patches is modeled as an environment for multi-agent system interactions suitable for deep reinforcement learning (DRL). ObjectAgents consists of two agents: a PositionFilterAgent (PFA) and a ScaleControlAgent (SCA). These two agents work together to complete the classification process by observing the state of the environment and taking their own actions. The PFA is responsible for selecting optimal candidate points in the image for classification, while the SCA identifies the ideal image scale for accurate object identification. By using a learning mechanism where the agents receive rewards based on how much their actions improve the classification performance, the framework optimizes and learns the best decision-making strategies through DRL, enhancing the decision-making process and leading to better generalization in complex remote sensing scenarios. Experiments on IowaNet (1 m), the ISPRS Vaihingen benchmark (9 cm), and a constructed Global Tidal Wetlands dataset (10 m) demonstrate that ObjectAgents consistently outperforms manually designed baselines and remains robust in complex heterogeneous regions.

title: Supervised Learning Approach to Infrared Image Based Condition Assessment of 33kV Lightning Surge Arresters
authors: Raveesha Illesinghe, Manjula Fernando, Nisal Paranawithana
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: This paper presents an online condition assessment method for 33kV Silicon rubber (SIR) housed ZnO surge arresters through infrared imaging. A total of 46 arrester samples including field-energized arresters, lab-tested arresters (field aged and a virgin arrester) were analyzed by infrared images and grouped into three "health" classes by laboratory measurements and modelling. Insulation resistance (IR) and leakage current (LC) measurements were taken for lab-tested arresters, whereas Finite Element Method (FEM) based modeling was used to assess field-energized samples. An infrared image consisted of 180 features and by Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), 180-dimensional thermal data were reduced to a 2D space with the three classification labels. Decision boundaries for classifying arresters were defined using Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) classifiers with their accuracy evaluated for effective arrester condition assessment.

title: Privacy-Preserving Heart Disease Prediction Using TabTransformer-Based Federated Learning on Non-IID Clinical Data
authors: Chamudika Pallewela
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: Heart disease remains a leading cause of mortality worldwide, and accurate early prediction is critical for timely clinical intervention. Traditional machine learning and deep learning approaches often rely on centralised patient data, raising significant concerns regarding privacy, security, and regulatory compliance. In this study, the researcher proposes a privacy-preserving federated learning framework that integrates a TabTransformer architecture with the FedProx optimisation algorithm to predict heart disease from tabular clinical data. The framework is specifically designed to address non-identically distributed (non-IID) client data across multiple healthcare institutions, ensuring stable model convergence while preserving data confidentiality. Comprehensive evaluations including accuracy, F1-score, precision, recall, ROC-AUC, and loss—demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms conventional federated models and achieves robust performance at both client and global levels. These findings underscore the potential of transformer-based federated learning as a scalable, privacy-preserving solution for heart disease prediction, paving the way for real-world deployment in clinical environments.In addition to demonstrating strong predictive accuracy, the proposed framework is benchmarked against baseline FL and centralised models, showing improved generalisation under heterogeneous conditions. A privacy-preserving protocol and interpretability analysis further highlight its practicality for real-world deployments.

title: Gaze for Dyads & Blinks for Triads: Task Engagement Predictors in Free-Flow VR Interactions
authors: Carlos Gonzalez Diaz, Georgiana Cristina Dobre, Elisabeth André, Cristina Conati
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and eXtended and Virtual Reality (AIxVR)
abstract: This paper investigates in an exploratory manner small group interactions in Social VR classroom conversations using eye-tracking data (group eye-gaze configurations, blink rate and blink duration) in terms of task engagement. Task engagement has been previously studied in educational settings as it is an important construct for learning. Prior research showed that eye signals in groups can assess rapport, attention, agreement or communication engagement. However, little research focused on whether eye-tracking data can predict task engagement in educational small group Social VR conversations. We conducted an exploratory study with 52 pedagogy university students (8 dyads, 12 triads) engaging in free-flow conversations on a given topic. Results indicated that eye-tracking data could predict task engagement using multiple linear regression analysis. However, group eye-gaze configurations were the significant predictors in dyads, whereas the blink rate was the strongest predictor associated with task engagement in triads. Based on these findings, we discuss the higher complexity in triadic gaze dynamics and why the blink rate was the task engagement predictor. On the other hand, the simplicity of dyadic gaze configurations might as well explain why it was found as a predictor. Furthermore, we argue that future work can explore machine learning detectors of task engagement dependent of group size. The results from this exploratory study help understanding non-verbal group interactions in VR and contribute to further work on engagement models for more socially-abled virtual experiences in Social VR applications.

title: Portable Near-Infrared Non-Invasive Imaging Tool for Early Detection of Oral Potentially Malignant Disorders
authors: R. A. I. A. Rajapaksha, Krishnasamy T., K. A. K. D. J. P. Jayasuriya, Uditha L. Wijewardhana, Umaya Balagalla, Sajith Edirisinghe
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: Oral potentially malignant disorders (OPMDs) are established precursors to oral cancer, and early identification greatly improves treatment outcomes. This study introduces the design, development, and preliminary validation of a portable, low-cost, and non-invasive near-infrared (NIR) imaging device tailored for point-of-care OPMD screening in resource-limited settings. The system combines a 940 nm NIR light source, a compact USB endoscope camera, an ESP32-C3 microcontroller, and a VL53L0X time-of-flight (ToF) sensor to maintain a consistent 2–3 cm imaging distance. Image acquisition is supported by a Python-based Flask/OpenCV pipeline that performs real-time illumination correction (Gaussian blur subtraction), local contrast enhancement using contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE), adaptive statistical thresholding, and morphological filtering, ultimately generating intuitive heatmaps of potential lesion sites. Bench tests confirmed stable hardware performance, robust distance control, and more than two hours of uninterrupted battery-powered operation. Initial qualitative evaluation by clinicians indicated enhanced lesion visibility in processed images compared to raw captures. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed device as an affordable and effective screening tool, with future work directed toward large-scale clinical trials and integration of machine learning for automated diagnosis.

title: MRCFSSA: A Multi-strategy Sparrow Search Algorithm for Robust Airport Gate Allocation in Dynamic IoT Environments
authors: Xinrui Gao, Yaohan Peng, Luying Chen, Zhuoning Zhu, Jianming Ding, Xiangbing Zhou, Wu Deng
abstract: In IoT-enabled airport ecosystems, the exponential growth of air traffic and the rapid development of smart terminals have intensified gate allocation challenges. Real-time data streams from IoT sensors require high-dimensional, dynamic decision-making under strict constraints. Conventional Sparrow Search Algorithm (SSA), though well-aligned with the gate allocation process, suffers from premature convergence and low computational efficiency, which limit its effectiveness in real-time optimization. To address these issues, this paper proposes a Multi-Strategy-Reinforced SSA (MRCFSSA) for airport gate allocation. Firstly, an adaptive distribution regulation strategy is designed to improve initial solutions. Then a dual-domain cooperative mutation strategy is proposed to enhance the global search and a stage-aware flight strategy is developed to dynamically balance the exploration and exploitation to prevent premature convergence. Finally, a comprehensive gate allocation method using MRCFSSA is proposed to balance gate idle time, minimize passenger walking distance, reduce remote gate usage, maximize large gate utilization and optimize flight-gate matching. The experiments based on real IoT-data show that MRCFSSA achieves gate allocation rate of 98% and improves convergence accuracy by 14.5%, which significantly enhance airport operation efficiency and passenger experience.

title: From Image Synthesis to Digital Twins: A Paradigm Shift in AI-Driven Medical Image Computing
authors: Xuhang Chen, Changhong Jing, Zhuo Li, Yanyan Shen, Guoheng Huang, Yan Liu, Baiying Lei, Chi-Man Pun, Shuqiang Wang
abstract: The integration of generative artificial intelligence (AI) in medical imaging represents a transformative paradigm shift from traditional discriminative models to sophisticated data synthesis frameworks, ultimately advancing toward the vision of patient-specific Digital Twins. This comprehensive review examines the technical evolution, clinical applications, and future perspectives of generative AI in medical image generation. The review categorizes clinical applications into three domains: (1) diagnostic enhancement through image quality improvement and cross-modal synthesis, (2) pathology simulation and prognostic prediction via longitudinal disease modeling, and (3) medical research and education through privacy-preserving data augmentation. We identify critical challenges impeding clinical translation, including limited evaluation metrics that fail to capture clinical validity, fairness and robustness issues arising from biased training data, lack of clinical interpretability in black-box models, and persistent privacy and security concerns. Looking forward, we envision a future generative medicine ecosystem built on three pillars: multi-omic holographic Digital Twins integrating imaging with genomic and clinical data, enhanced human-machine collaboration paradigms, and comprehensive regulatory frameworks. This review provides essential insights for researchers and clinicians working to harness generative AI’s potential while addressing the fundamental challenges required for its responsible clinical deployment.
